title: Homer Badman

jokes.text: Damn FDA. Why can't it all be marshmallow?
jokes.setup: Homer examining cereal, presumably Lucky Charms with its mix of marshmallows and grain pieces
jokes.punchline: His childish frustration with government regulation of candy ratios in cereal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - blaming the FDA for cereal composition. Great character integration showing his childish food obsessions.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why do they have to regulate how many marshmallows are in my cereal? It's tyranny! Pure tyranny! I want a bowl that's 90% marshmallows and 10% regret!

jokes.text: Bart, don't put the non-marshmallow pieces back in the box. They go in the trash.
jokes.setup: Marge giving parenting instructions about cereal consumption
jokes.punchline: She's enabling the family's complete rejection of nutrition
jokes.explanation: Marge's matter-of-fact tone about wasteful, unhealthy behavior is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Now children, when eating cereal, it's important to use moderation and remember that it's part of a balanced breakfast.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Balanced? Marge, I'm eating five bowls a day. That's balance!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's not what I meant. You're supposed to eat healthy foods too.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Mom, the box says it has vitamins!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well... I suppose if it says so on the box, it must be true. Have as much as you want, sweetie.

jokes.text: You like sweets, kids? I know a place that's sweeter than sweetness itself. In this sweet place, earthly doughnuts are sour as poison. You'd spit them out, you would. I'm talking about the Candy Industry Trade Show.
jokes.setup: Homer building mystical, almost religious reverence for a magical sweet place
jokes.punchline: It's just a trade show - deflation from mystical to mundane
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with Homer's over-the-top spiritual candy rhetoric building to a mundane reveal. Great structure and character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... a magical place of pure sweetness and wonder...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Where all my dreams come true...

dialogue.speaker: Man
dialogue.line: Sir, it's just a candy trade show.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: They hid them in every millionth Krusty Klump Bar... and Krusty Klump Bar with almonds.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining how he got rare tickets to the candy show
jokes.punchline: The separate mention of almond bars as if they're completely different products
jokes.explanation: Mild humor in the redundant specificity typical of promotional fine print.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I won these tickets fair and square at the radio station. They had a contest: name all the different kinds of candy bars. I got them all - Snickers, Milky Way, Butterfinger, Almond Joy, Mounds, Twix, Three Musketeers, PayDay, 100 Grand, Nestle Crunch, Baby Ruth... and almond bars.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, almond bars aren't a real candy bar.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I thought they were!

jokes.text: Hey. Hey. I have asked you nicely not to mangle my merchandise. You leave me no choice but to ask you nicely again.
jokes.setup: Store clerk confronting destructive customer
jokes.punchline: His ultimate threat is just asking nicely again - complete non-escalation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Apu character moment - polite to a fault. Excellent structure with 'you leave me no choice' setting up expectation for escalation, then complete deflation. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Please, sir, if you do not stop destroying my merchandise, I will have to ask you very nicely to stop.

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: And if that does not work, I will ask you again, but with more conviction in my voice.

jokes.text: Marge, they can't carry enough candy. They have puny little muscles. Not big, ropy ones like you.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining why Marge should come instead of the kids
jokes.punchline: His 'compliment' focuses on Marge's physical strength in unflattering terms
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer tactlessness - trying to compliment Marge but making her sound masculine. Good cringe humor.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, you should go instead of the kids. You've got the upper body strength of a linebacker and the endurance of a pack mule. Plus, your hair can hold supplies.

jokes.text: You go, Mom. For the greater good. - For the greater good.
jokes.setup: Kids encouraging their mother to help with candy collection
jokes.punchline: They solemnly invoke 'the greater good' for personal candy acquisition
jokes.explanation: Kids' mock-serious tone about candy gathering is amusingly self-serving.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Come on, Mom. Help us out here.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It's for the greater good.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: The greater good.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: The greater good?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The greater good.

jokes.text: They wouldn't be if you would sit in a hollowed-out wheelchair.
jokes.setup: Marge questioning if all the pockets on Homer's outfit are necessary
jokes.punchline: Homer's alternative is even more extreme and absurd
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist escalation - Homer's solution to too many pockets is disability fraud. Concise and perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, do you really need all those pockets?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: All those pockets? Marge, you don't understand. Without these pockets, I'd have to carry everything in my hands. Or worse... I'd have to remember where I put things.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, maybe you could just—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, no, no. The alternative is clear. I'd need to sew pockets onto my underwear. And then my socks. And then my OTHER socks. Pretty soon I'd be nothing but pockets with a man attached to them, and Marge, I'm not ready for that kind of responsibility.

jokes.text: I had to choose between a grad student at the university or a scary-looking hobo.
jokes.setup: Marge explaining difficulty finding a babysitter
jokes.punchline: These were her only two options, presented as equivalent choices
jokes.explanation: Sets up the contrast between the two babysitter options effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I can't find a babysitter. My only two options are either Barney or a rabid dog.

jokes.text: Please, the hobo. Please, the hobo. The hobo.
jokes.setup: Bart hearing about babysitter options
jokes.punchline: He desperately wants the scary hobo over the responsible grad student
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart behavior - choosing chaos over supervision. The repetition adds desperation.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: We found you a new babysitter. She's a very responsible graduate student.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: A graduate student? What about that scary hobo who hangs around the Kwik-E-Mart?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's a great idea! He owes me money anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah! The hobo! Please, anyone but the grad student!

jokes.text: Ashley Grant. You gave a talk on women's issues at my school... on how we don't have to be second-class citizens. Mom, how can you leave us with this maniac?
jokes.setup: Ashley introducing herself as a feminist advocate
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately calls the women's rights advocate a maniac
jokes.explanation: Good ironic contrast between Ashley's credentials and Bart's immediate dismissal.

dialogue.speaker: Ashley
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Ashley. I'm a feminist advocate and women's rights activist.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: You're a maniac!

jokes.text: Hurry. If we're early, we can get a picture... with the two surviving Musketeers.
jokes.setup: Homer excited about celebrity photo opportunity at candy show
jokes.punchline: Implies the third Musketeer has died, making it darkly specific
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about candy mascot mortality. Unexpectedly morbid detail in Homer's excitement.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A photo with the Nesquik Bunny! This is the greatest day of my life!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, where are the other two musketeers?

dialogue.speaker: Nesquik Bunny
dialogue.line: There are only two of us now.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh my God, one of the musketeers is dead!

jokes.text: So you're one of those 'don't-call-me-a-chick' chicks, huh?
jokes.setup: Bart addressing the feminist babysitter
jokes.punchline: He uses the word 'chicks' while acknowledging she doesn't like being called that
jokes.explanation: Bart's deliberate provocation shows his mischievous nature. Efficient wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I know you don't like being called 'chicks', but the baby chicks in the yard are getting away!

jokes.text: Disemboweler IV. The game where condemned criminals dig at each other with rusty hooks.
jokes.setup: Ashley showing Bart a video game to get him to do housework
jokes.punchline: The game's premise is extremely violent and specific
jokes.explanation: Absurd video game violence parody. The specificity of 'rusty hooks' adds to the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Ashley
dialogue.line: Look Bart, I got you a video game where you play as a teenager who has to do chores or his parents will ground him!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wow, that sounds... incredibly boring.

dialogue.speaker: Ashley
dialogue.line: Wait, there's more! If you don't do the dishes, your mom hits you with a spatula. If you don't vacuum, your dad chases you with a belt. And if you don't take out the trash, they lock you in the basement!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Okay, now we're talking!

jokes.text: See, Lisa? Male's aren't hard to tame. They all follow their video cartridges.
jokes.setup: Ashley successfully bribing Bart with video game time
jokes.punchline: Her feminist generalization about male behavior, plus saying 'cartridges'
jokes.explanation: Mild humor in the condescending generalization and dated gaming terminology.

dialogue.speaker: Ashley
dialogue.line: See? All men are easily manipulated with simple rewards. It's like you're all just dogs chasing a ball.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey! That's not fair!

dialogue.speaker: Ashley
dialogue.line: Oh please, you were literally distracted by shiny cartridges five seconds ago.

jokes.text: I feel like a kid in some kind of a store.
jokes.setup: Homer overwhelmed by the candy trade show
jokes.punchline: Butchers the common phrase 'kid in a candy store' when literally in a candy show
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer malapropism. The irony of getting the phrase wrong while literally living it makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm like a candy store in a kid!

jokes.text: Well, as you can see, I have created a lemon ball so sour... it can only be safely contained in a magnetic field.
jokes.setup: Scientist presenting his candy creation
jokes.punchline: Treating candy like dangerous nuclear material requiring containment
jokes.explanation: Absurd scientific approach to candy making. Good escalation of the sourness concept.

dialogue.speaker: Candy Scientist
dialogue.line: Behold! My latest creation - a candy so potent, it requires triple-layered lead shielding and can only be handled with tongs by personnel wearing hazmat suits.

dialogue.speaker: Candy Scientist
dialogue.line: One lick has been known to cause spontaneous combustion, temporary levitation, and an inexplicable craving for more candy.

dialogue.speaker: Candy Scientist
dialogue.line: We've had to relocate the research facility three times after the previous test subjects achieved nuclear fission.

dialogue.speaker: Candy Scientist
dialogue.line: But it's fruity!

jokes.text: Like what? - One, a humorous substitute for your own lips. - Keep going. - Two... I'm needed in the basement.
jokes.setup: Salesman claiming wax lips have 1000 uses, Homer demanding specifics
jokes.punchline: After one use, he panics and flees rather than continue
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of the ridiculous marketing claim. The excuse is transparently fake.

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, wax lips have 1000 uses!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Name one.

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: Well, you can eat them...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's one. What else?

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: Uh... well...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Name another!

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: You can... wear them as lips.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's basically the same thing.

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: Well, I... uh...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Come on! What's use number three?

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: I... I don't... AHHH!

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: I can't do this anymore!

jokes.text: You're gonna have to put some sugar on that celery or get out, ma'am.
jokes.setup: Someone apparently brought celery to a candy convention
jokes.punchline: The absurd compromise solution and the threat of ejection over vegetables
jokes.explanation: The sugar-on-celery compromise and security threat is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Vendor
dialogue.line: No vegetables at the Candy Convention!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But I brought celery!

dialogue.speaker: Vendor
dialogue.line: Okay, you can stay, but the celery has to go in a candy wrapper.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Deal!

dialogue.speaker: Vendor
dialogue.line: And if I catch you eating that celery without chocolate on it, you're out of here!

jokes.text: The gummi Venus de Milo, carved by gummi artisans... who work exclusively in the medium of gummi.
jokes.setup: Presenting the ultimate gummi candy
jokes.punchline: Treating gummi as a serious artistic medium with dedicated artisans
jokes.explanation: Absurd elevation of candy to high art. The redundant 'gummi' emphasizes the ridiculousness.

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: Behold, the Gummi Venus de Milo! Carved from a single, preciously type gummi bear.

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: Priceless.

jokes.text: Will you two stop saying 'gummi' so much?
jokes.setup: Characters repeatedly saying 'gummi' in the previous exchange
jokes.punchline: Marge breaks the fourth wall to complain about the repetitive dialogue
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-joke acknowledging the repetitive word usage. Marge as voice of reason commenting on the writing itself.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: How many times are we going to say 'gummi' in this scene?

jokes.text: How did they get there?
jokes.setup: Marge presumably sees Homer has somehow acquired the gummi Venus
jokes.punchline: Her amazement at Homer's impossible theft/acquisition
jokes.explanation: Visual gag reaction - implies Homer somehow stole the candy. Mild setup for his theft.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer! How did you get the gummi Venus de Milo?!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I found it.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: You found it? Homer, that's on display at the museum!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: Halt! Halt! - Run, Marge. Save the booty.
jokes.setup: Security chasing Homer for theft
jokes.punchline: Homer tells Marge to save the 'booty' (pirate term for treasure/candy)
jokes.explanation: Homer treating candy theft like piracy. 'Booty' works both as pirate treasure and becomes relevant later for different reasons.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge! Save the booty!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: The booty?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The candy, woman!

jokes.text: See you in hell, candy boy!
jokes.setup: Homer escaping from candy trade show security
jokes.punchline: Dramatically over-the-top threat to candy security guard
jokes.explanation: Homer's melodramatic escape line. Treating candy theft like a action movie betrayal.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You'll never take me alive, copper!

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: Sir, please step away from the candy display.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm warning you! I've got a sugar rush and I'm not afraid to use it!

jokes.text: Okay, don't panic. She can't have gotten far. She has no arms.
jokes.setup: Homer realizing his gummi Venus de Milo is missing
jokes.punchline: He applies literal logic about the statue's missing arms to tracking it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - treating a candy statue like it could walk away. Perfect character integration and economy. The Venus de Milo's missing arms become a tactical disadvantage.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My gummi Venus de Milo! She's gone! Oh, why does this always happen to me?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... if she's missing her arms, then she can't have stolen herself. So someone else must have taken her!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm a genius!

jokes.text: Visual gag: Ashley trapped/uncomfortable in the car for 20 minutes while Homer searches
jokes.setup: Homer tells Marge Ashley is fine in the car
jokes.punchline: Cut to Ashley clearly not having a good time
jokes.explanation: Visual contradiction of Homer's claim. Sets up the later misunderstanding but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ashley is fine in the car.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: Cut to Ashley clearly not having a good time.

jokes.text: How come you can go to the moon, but can't make my shoes smell good?
jokes.setup: Homer talking to Ashley about scientific progress
jokes.punchline: His priorities focus on personal shoe odor over space exploration
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer misplaced priorities - space program vs. foot odor. Good observational comedy about technology gaps.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, Ashley, science has made incredible progress. We've got space exploration, medical breakthroughs, all kinds of amazing stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But you want to know what really matters? Getting rid of the smell in my shoes.

jokes.text: Could you take the wheel for a second? I have to scratch in two places at once.
jokes.setup: Homer asking the babysitter to drive
jokes.punchline: His reason is gross personal hygiene needs
jokes.explanation: Homer's complete lack of social awareness. The specificity of 'two places at once' makes it worse.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, can you drive me somewhere?

dialogue.speaker: Babysitter
dialogue.line: Sure, where?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: To the store. I need to buy some soap, deodorant, and a new shirt. I haven't showered in three weeks.

jokes.text: Thank you.
jokes.setup: Ashley presumably hands Homer the gummi Venus that was stuck to her
jokes.punchline: Homer's polite thanks for what will be perceived as sexual harassment
jokes.explanation: The innocent politeness that will be completely misunderstood. Sets up the main conflict.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you!

jokes.text: There he is. There's the man that sexually harassed me.
jokes.setup: Ashley making a public accusation against Homer
jokes.punchline: The complete misunderstanding of Homer's innocent candy retrieval
jokes.explanation: The central misunderstanding that drives the episode. Strong dramatic irony.

dialogue.speaker: Ashley
dialogue.line: Homer Simpson tried to grab my candy!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I was just trying to get a piece of candy that fell on the ground!

dialogue.speaker: Ashley
dialogue.line: He grabbed me!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what is she talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I didn't grab her! I grabbed the candy!

jokes.text: For a minute there I thought I was in big trouble. It's just a...
jokes.setup: Homer hearing the accusation and starting to dismiss it
jokes.punchline: His relief is completely misplaced - he IS in big trouble
jokes.explanation: Homer's obliviousness to the severity of his situation. Good dramatic irony.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Me? In big trouble? That's ridiculous!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you burned down the nuclear plant.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, thank God. I thought you found out about the donuts I stole from Lard Lad.

jokes.text: Two, four, six, eight Homer's crime was very great. 'Great' meaning 'large or immense' We use it in the pejorative sense
jokes.setup: Protesters chanting against Homer
jokes.punchline: They stop to clarify their word usage mid-chant
jokes.explanation: Brilliant interruption of protest rhythm for pedantic clarification. Very quotable and unexpectedly intellectual.

dialogue.speaker: Protesters
dialogue.line: Homer Simpson is a big fat pig!

dialogue.speaker: Protester
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. By 'pig' we mean a filthy, disgusting slob, not an actual swine.

dialogue.speaker: Protesters
dialogue.line: Homer Simpson is a big fat pig!

jokes.text: I can't even say the word 'titmouse' without giggling like a schoolgirl.
jokes.setup: Homer defending his bashful nature to Marge
jokes.punchline: He proves his point by giggling at the innocent bird name
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - his juvenile mind finding humor in innocent words. Self-proving statement.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not bashful, Marge. I'm just... selective about what I find funny.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you giggle at everything.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's not true. I have a very sophisticated sense of humor.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah. For example, I would never laugh at something as silly as... as a bird called a 'titmouse.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: *giggling uncontrollably* Tit... mouse... hehehehe

jokes.text: I was grabbing a gummi Venus de Milo that got stuck to your pants. - Yeah, right. That's the oldest excuse in the book.
jokes.setup: Homer giving his truthful explanation
jokes.punchline: Ashley dismisses the truth as an obviously fake excuse
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony - the truth sounds like a ridiculous lie. 'Oldest excuse in the book' is absurd for this specific situation.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I was late because I got stuck in traffic on the way home from work.

dialogue.speaker: Ashley
dialogue.line: Yeah, right. That's the oldest excuse in the book.

jokes.text: Why would anybody wanna touch a girl's butt? That's where cooties come from.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to understand the accusation against Homer
jokes.punchline: His childish logic about cooties as the reason to avoid sexual harassment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart perspective on adult behavior through childhood lens. Innocent take on serious topic.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're telling me you can get in trouble for touching someone without permission?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's right, boy.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But I thought that's what cooties were for! To keep girls away so you don't accidentally touch 'em and get all mushy!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Son, that's not how this works...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So you're saying cooties are a lie? What's next, you gonna tell me Santa isn't real?

jokes.text: Lisa, remember that postcard Grampa sent us from Florida... of that alligator biting that woman's bottom? - Oh, yeah. That was brilliant. - That's right. We all thought it was hilarious. But it turns out we were wrong. That alligator was sexually harassing that woman.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to explain sexual harassment to his kids using a postcard
jokes.punchline: He treats a novelty postcard alligator as a real sexual predator
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - retrofitting childhood humor as serious social commentary. Homer's earnest reinterpretation is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: See kids, sexual harassment is when someone does something to you that you don't want them to do. Like this alligator here — he's always touching me inappropriately.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's just a postcard.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know what I felt, Lisa. And this alligator felt it too.

jokes.text: And the dog in the Coppertone ad? Same deal? - Well, that's kind of a gray area.
jokes.setup: Lisa extending Homer's logic to another advertising image
jokes.punchline: Homer seriously considers the legal implications of the Coppertone dog
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the absurd logic. Homer's hesitation about the 'gray area' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, by your logic, shouldn't you be concerned about the Coppertone dog pulling down that girl's bathing suit? Isn't that a crime?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hmm... you know, that's a good point. I never thought about the legal implications. That dog could be charged with indecent exposure or sexual assault.

jokes.text: We ain't crazy about nuclear power either!
jokes.setup: Protesters outside Homer's house for sexual harassment
jokes.punchline: They randomly add nuclear power to their grievances
jokes.explanation: Protester mission creep - adding unrelated causes to their focus. Shows how protests can lose focus.

dialogue.speaker: Protesters
dialogue.line: Nuclear power! Nuclear power!

jokes.text: You mean, I'm on my own? I've never been on my own. Oh, no. On own. On own. I need help. Oh, God, help me. Help me, God!
jokes.setup: Homer realizing Marge can't fix his situation
jokes.punchline: Complete mental breakdown over having to handle something himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - his complete inability to handle independence. The repetitive panic is well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge can't fix this? But... but she fixes everything! My socks, my shirts, my life choices!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I have to do it myself? MYSELF?! No! No, no, no, no, no!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why would you do this to me, Marge? I'm not equipped for this! I'm not equipped for anything!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm going to die! We're all going to die!

jokes.text: Hello, Homer, this is God... frey Jones from the TV magazine show Rock Bottom.
jokes.setup: Voice claiming to be God responding to Homer's prayers
jokes.punchline: Actually a TV producer with a name that sounds like God
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with the pause after 'God.' Perfect timing and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Godfrey Jones
dialogue.line: Hello, Homer. I'm Godfrey Jones, a television producer. I've been listening to your prayers.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: Godfrey Jones
dialogue.line: No, no. Just Godfrey.

jokes.text: I saw that report you did on Sasquatch. It was fair and even-handed.
jokes.setup: Homer agreeing to the TV interview
jokes.punchline: He praises tabloid journalism about a mythical creature as 'fair and even-handed'
jokes.explanation: Homer's naive trust in sensationalist media. His criteria for good journalism is hilariously misguided.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fair and even-handed? That's the most honest journalism I've ever seen! Those tabloids really know how to report the facts about Bigfoot.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: Homer, those stories aren't real.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, they're real enough for me!

jokes.text: Oh, just thinking about that sweet, sweet candy... I just wish I had another one right now.
jokes.setup: Homer describing his innocent candy retrieval
jokes.punchline: His language sounds sexual when taken out of context
jokes.explanation: Sets up the selective editing perfectly. Homer's innocent candy obsession becomes ammunition.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I was just trying to get a piece of candy from the machine, and I had to get down on my knees and really work it with my hands, stroking it back and forth, back and forth, getting it nice and wet with my tongue...

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I was talking about the candy machine!

jokes.text: Say, can you introduce me to the Sasquatch? I like his style.
jokes.setup: Homer finishing his interview
jokes.punchline: He thinks Sasquatch is a real person he could meet
jokes.explanation: Homer's continued delusion about tabloid TV reality. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So, in conclusion, I believe Sasquatch is out there somewhere, and one day I'm gonna meet him. We'll probably become best friends. Maybe go bowling.

jokes.text: I keep telling you, I just grow sorghum here. - And where are the hookers? - Around back.
jokes.setup: Reporter investigating supposed sex farm
jokes.punchline: Innocent farmer accidentally confirms the story
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with the farmer accidentally providing evidence for what he's denying.

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: So you're denying these allegations of a sex farm?

dialogue.speaker: Farmer
dialogue.line: That's right. We're a legitimate agricultural operation.

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: Then how do you explain the suspicious activity at night?

dialogue.speaker: Farmer
dialogue.line: Well, we breed our animals after dark when it's cooler. It's more humane.

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: And what exactly are you breeding?

dialogue.speaker: Farmer
dialogue.line: Mostly sheep and goats. Sometimes horses if the price is right.

jokes.text: Somebody had to take the babysitter home. Then I noticed she was sitting on her sweet can... I grabbed her sweet can... Oh, just thinking about her can... I just wish I had her sweet, sweet, sweet can.
jokes.setup: Homer's innocent interview about candy
jokes.punchline: Malicious editing removes 'gummi' context to make it sound sexual
jokes.explanation: Brilliant demonstration of how editing can completely reverse meaning - sharp media satire.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I love gummi bears. Gummi bears are great. I eat gummi bears all day long.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: So Homer, you're saying you enjoy... bears all day long?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's right!

jokes.text: Somebody had to take the babysitter home. Then I noticed she was sitting on her sweet can... I grabbed her sweet can... Oh, just thinking about her can... I just wish I had her sweet, sweet, sweet can.
jokes.setup: Homer's interview being selectively edited by the show
jokes.punchline: Innocent candy references edited to sound sexual
jokes.explanation: Brilliant media manipulation satire. Shows how editing can completely change meaning. Excellent dramatic irony.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I love licking the wrapper.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: Homer Simpson admits to a fetish.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sometimes I eat a whole box in one sitting.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: He consumed an entire box in a single session.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The strawberry one is my favorite.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: When asked about his preference, Homer chose the strawberry variety.

jokes.text: Dramatization: May not have happened.
jokes.setup: Tabloid TV show depicting dramatic recreation
jokes.punchline: Disclaimer admitting the scene is probably fake
jokes.explanation: Perfect satire of tabloid TV disclaimers. The casual 'may not have happened' is brilliantly dismissive.

dialogue.speaker: TV Disclaimer
dialogue.line: The following scene is probably fake.

jokes.text: Under the sea
jokes.setup: Homer's solution to his problems
jokes.punchline: He literally wants to live underwater like a Disney movie
jokes.explanation: Homer's childish fantasy solution. References The Little Mermaid as a serious life plan.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I've got it! I'll move us all underwater!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's insane.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No it's not! We can be like The Little Mermaid. I'll be Prince Eric, you'll be Ariel, and the kids can be... fish or whatever.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's not how biology works.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Who cares? Underwater, nobody can find me and ask me to do stuff!

jokes.text: Under the sea... There'll be no accusations Just friendly crustaceans Under the sea
jokes.setup: Homer singing about underwater life
jokes.punchline: Little Mermaid parody replacing romance with legal problems and sea creature friendship
jokes.explanation: Competent Disney parody adapting 'Under the Sea' to Homer's specific predicament.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: 🎵 I want to be where the people aren't, I want to see, want to see 'em, wantin'... I want to walk around like, talk like I'm not me. 🎵

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: 🎵 Up where they walk, up where they run, up where they sue all day in the sun, wanderin' free—wish I could be part of that lawsuit... 🎵

jokes.text: That's your solution to everything, to move under the sea. It won't happen. - Not with that attitude.
jokes.setup: Marge dismissing Homer's underwater plan
jokes.punchline: Homer treats it as a motivational failure, not a physical impossibility
jokes.explanation: Homer's delusional optimism about impossible solutions. Suggests this isn't his first underwater proposal.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you can't breathe underwater.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not with that attitude, I can't.

jokes.text: Homer sleeps nude in an oxygen tent he believes gives him sexual powers.
jokes.setup: TV news reporting on Homer
jokes.punchline: Bizarre personal detail that's both specific and weird
jokes.explanation: Absurd and specific personal detail. The oxygen tent sexual powers is a perfect Homer delusion.

jokes.text: Hey, that's a half-truth.
jokes.setup: Homer hearing the news report about his oxygen tent
jokes.punchline: He objects to the accuracy, not the invasion of privacy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response - confirming part of the weird story instead of denying it entirely. Great character logic.

dialogue.speaker: News Reporter
dialogue.line: We interrupt this program to bring you a special news report. Homer Simpson is in an oxygen tent and he's gone bonkers.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, I'm not bonkers!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And for the record, I've been in better tents.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, I cant go on. - That's okay. Your tears say more than real evidence ever could.
jokes.setup: Woman breaking down during TV interview about Homer
jokes.punchline: Host explicitly values emotion over facts
jokes.explanation: Sharp media satire about emotion over evidence. The phrase 'tears say more than real evidence' is perfectly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Random Woman
dialogue.line: Homer Simpson destroyed my life! He ruined everything!

dialogue.speaker: TV Host
dialogue.line: That's wonderful! Can you cry a little harder for the camera?

jokes.text: And here's your host, Gentle Ben.
jokes.setup: Introduction for a talk show host
jokes.punchline: Host is presumably the bear from the TV show Gentle Ben
jokes.explanation: Absurd visual gag of a bear hosting a talk show. References classic TV show.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, please welcome our special guest tonight...

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: He's a star of television, a gentle soul, and a credit to his species...

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Put your hands together for Ben!

jokes.text: Let's have less Homer Simpsons and more money for public schools.
jokes.setup: Woman speaking on the Gentle Ben show
jokes.punchline: Randomly connects Homer scandal to education funding
jokes.explanation: Random political pivot typical of talk show segments. Connects unrelated issues.

dialogue.speaker: Woman on Ben
dialogue.line: We need to increase education funding in this state.

dialogue.speaker: Woman on Ben
dialogue.line: Just last week, Homer Simpson was caught doing something absolutely ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Woman on Ben
dialogue.line: If that's not a reason to invest more in our schools, I don't know what is.

jokes.text: Homer S: Portrait of an Ass-Grabber. Starring Dennis Franz.
jokes.setup: TV movie adaptation of Homer's scandal
jokes.punchline: Casting Dennis Franz (known for playing crude characters) as Homer
jokes.explanation: Good media escalation satire. Dennis Franz casting is perfect for crude TV movie.

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: Coming this fall, a TV movie adaptation of Homer Simpson's scandal. Starring Dennis Franz as Homer.

jokes.text: No, Mr. Simpson. That's sexual harassment. If you keep it up, I'll yell so loud, the whole country will hear. - With a man in the White House? Not likely.
jokes.setup: Dramatized scene of the harassment
jokes.punchline: TV Homer makes a political joke about presidential scandals
jokes.explanation: Contemporary political reference about Clinton scandals. Shows how the TV movie exaggerates Homer's character.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Hey, at least I'm not as bad as those presidents with their scandals!

jokes.text: With a man in the White House? Not likely.
jokes.setup: Threatening that harassment complaint will be ignored
jokes.punchline: Implies presidential administration won't care about women's complaints
jokes.explanation: Political satire about government indifference to harassment complaints works for the era.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You think anyone's gonna believe a harassment complaint against me? Have you seen who's in the White House?

jokes.text: Now, this technology is new to me... but I'm pretty sure that's Homer Simpson in the oven... rotating slowly. His body temperature has risen to over 400 degrees. He's literally stewing in his own juices.
jokes.setup: Reporter using thermal imaging to spy on Homer
jokes.punchline: Misinterprets normal cooking as Homer literally roasting
jokes.explanation: Absurd misinterpretation of thermal imaging technology. 'Stewing in his own juices' is both literal and metaphorical.

dialogue.speaker: TV Reporter
dialogue.line: According to thermal imaging, we can see Homer Simpson is... oh my God, he's roasting himself alive!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: I'm just making a sandwich!

jokes.text: Of course, this is just a television poll... which is not legally binding, unless Proposition 304 passes. And we all pray it will.
jokes.setup: Reporter explaining TV poll results about Homer
jokes.punchline: Suggests making TV polls legally binding through legislation
jokes.explanation: Absurd idea of giving legal weight to TV polls. Good satire of media influence on democracy.

dialogue.speaker: TV Reporter
dialogue.line: Our latest poll shows that Homer Simpson is the least popular man in Springfield. We should make TV polls legally binding.

jokes.text: We got the real dirt on Homer Simpson, and the bidding starts at 10 G's. - I bid 10 G's. - Ten-five G's.
jokes.setup: Homer's friends offering to sell information about him
jokes.punchline: They betray him immediately and reporters bid against each other
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing how fair-weather Homer's friends are. Auction format escalates quickly.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: We'll sell your secrets to the highest bidder.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Yeah, Homer, you're going down!

dialogue.speaker: TV Reporter 1
dialogue.line: Five thousand dollars!

dialogue.speaker: TV Reporter 2
dialogue.line: Ten thousand!

dialogue.speaker: TV Reporter 1
dialogue.line: Fifteen thousand!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Do I hear twenty?

jokes.text: Sorry, Dad. We do believe in you. We really do. It's just hard not to listen to TV. It's spent so much more time raising us than you.
jokes.setup: Bart explaining why the kids doubted Homer
jokes.punchline: TV has been more of a parent than Homer himself
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy about parenting and media influence. Devastatingly honest while being funny. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: TV has been more of a parent than Homer himself

jokes.text: Maybe TV is right. - TV's always right.
jokes.setup: Homer starting to doubt himself
jokes.punchline: Lisa's matter-of-fact agreement about TV infallibility
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the TV parenting joke. Lisa's deadpan delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, maybe I'm not as smart as I think I am.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, you once thought the TV was a window to another dimension.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, it is!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No, it's not.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, the TV said it was, so...

jokes.text: And the number one reference that I am running into the ground is: Homer Simpson.
jokes.setup: Late night talk show host doing his top ten list
jokes.punchline: Admitting he's overusing Homer as material
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about comedy writers overusing topical material. References Letterman's actual style.

dialogue.speaker: David Letterman
dialogue.line: Number three: I've been on the air for twenty years and I've only made fun of Homer Simpson like five thousand times.

dialogue.speaker: David Letterman
dialogue.line: That's not overusing material, that's just... Homer.

jokes.text: I think about weird stuff. Like, what would happen if E.T. And Mr. T had a baby? Well, you'd get Mr. E.T., wouldn't you? And, you know, I think he'd sound a little something like this: 'I pity the fool who doesn't phone home.'
jokes.setup: Comedian setting up hypothetical celebrity mashup
jokes.punchline: Combining E.T.'s famous line with Mr. T's catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Standard 80s stand-up premise mixing pop culture references - competent but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Evening at the Improv Comedian
dialogue.line: You know what would be funny? If E.T. and Mr. T had a baby, right? And the baby's first words were: 'E.T. phone home, fool!'

jokes.text: Like, what would happen if E.T. and Mr. T had a baby? Well, you'd get Mr. E.T., wouldn't you? And, you know, I think he'd sound a little something like this: 'I pity the fool who doesn't phone home.'
jokes.setup: Comedian doing observational humor mixing two pop culture figures
jokes.punchline: Combines E.T.'s catchphrase with Mr. T's catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Generic pop culture mashup comedy. Low craft but demonstrates the weak material Homer prefers.

dialogue.speaker: Comedian
dialogue.line: E.T. phone home... I pity the fool who doesn't answer!

jokes.text: There are only 49 stars on that flag. I'll be deep in the cold, cold ground before I recognize Missouri.
jokes.setup: Grandpa commenting on American flag in public access studio
jokes.punchline: His bizarre personal vendetta against Missouri statehood
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grandpa - arbitrary, passionate hatred of specific state with dramatic death vow.

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: That's the American flag. I fought for that flag. Well, I fought against it in World War I, but I was on the right side... I think.

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: You know what I hate about that flag? Missouri. Dang Missouri thinking it can just become a state in 1821. Well, I'm still mad about it!

jokes.text: Hello. I am Homer Simpson. Or, as some of you wags have dubbed me, 'Father Goose.'
jokes.setup: Homer starting his public access show
jokes.punchline: He misunderstands his nickname, making it sound endearing
jokes.explanation: Homer's misinterpretation of his derogatory nickname as affectionate. Good character delusion.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Welcome to my public access show. You know, people have been calling me 'Homer the Great' all week.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: At first I thought they said 'Homer the Grate' — you know, like a cheese grater. I was offended! But then I realized they said 'Great'!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Although, come to think of it, 'Homer the Grate' does have a nice ring to it. It's kind of endearing, really.

jokes.text: Like one time, we were having this race with this stupid, old-timey bicycle... with the big wheel in front. So I figure, 'We'll see about that.' So I get this big chunk of cinderblock, and I...
jokes.setup: Homer trying to prove his innocence by admitting other wrongdoing
jokes.punchline: He starts confessing to sabotaging a bicycle race before cutting himself off
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - proving innocence by confessing to other crimes. Self-awareness kicks in just in time.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I didn't do it! But I did sabotage that bicycle race in '87, and— wait, forget I said that.

jokes.text: Hello? No, Janice doesn't live here. - Hello? Yes, I am interested in long-distance savings. Very interested.
jokes.setup: Worker claiming the phones are lighting up with support
jokes.punchline: Both calls are wrong numbers and telemarketers
jokes.explanation: Deflates the 'phones lighting up' cliche. Shows the pathetic reality of public access TV.

dialogue.speaker: TV Station Worker
dialogue.line: The phones are lighting up with support!

dialogue.speaker: TV Station Worker
dialogue.line: First call is a wrong number.

dialogue.speaker: TV Station Worker
dialogue.line: Second call is a telemarketer.

jokes.text: Marge, this is so depressing. My only hope is this homemade Prozac. - Needs more ice cream.
jokes.setup: Homer's depression over his situation
jokes.punchline: His 'medication' is just ice cream, and it needs more ice cream
jokes.explanation: Good Homer self-medication joke. His approach to depression is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My depression is so bad, I need medication.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Good thing I found some.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... ice cream.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, it's not working. I need more ice cream!

jokes.text: I didn't come forward because in this country... it makes you look like a pervert. But every single Scottish person does it!
jokes.setup: Willie explaining why he didn't reveal his video evidence
jokes.punchline: His defense that all Scottish people secretly videotape couples
jokes.explanation: Perfect Willie character moment - bizarre cultural defense. The Scottish stereotype twist is unexpected and absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Well, ye see, all us Scottish folk do it! We videotape couples in the bushes. It's in our blood, laddie!

jokes.text: You know, the courts might not work anymore... but as long as everybody is videotaping everyone else... justice will be done.
jokes.setup: Lisa commenting on Willie's video evidence clearing Homer
jokes.punchline: Surveillance state as replacement for judicial system
jokes.explanation: Prescient commentary on surveillance culture. Lisa's matter-of-fact delivery of a dystopian observation.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, I guess that's what happens when we replace our entire judicial system with a surveillance state.

jokes.text: You know, the courts might not work anymore... but as long as everybody is videotaping everyone else... justice will be done.
jokes.setup: Lisa commenting on Willie's surveillance footage clearing Homer
jokes.punchline: Optimistic view of surveillance state as replacement for failed legal system
jokes.explanation: Prescient commentary on surveillance culture as accidental justice mechanism - very forward-looking.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose if we can't trust the justice system, at least we can trust an omnipresent surveillance state to keep us safe.

jokes.text: Homer, I thought you were an animal. But your daughter said you were a decent man. - I guess she was right. - You're both right.
jokes.setup: Ashley apologizing to Homer after seeing the video evidence
jokes.punchline: Homer agrees that he's both an animal AND a decent man
jokes.explanation: Homer accepts both contradictory descriptions. Perfect character moment of self-awareness without shame.

dialogue.speaker: Ashley
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I saw the video evidence.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's right. I'm both an animal AND a decent man.

jokes.text: Wow, V8 juice isn't one-eighth gasoline. - And Ted Koppel is a robot.
jokes.setup: TV show making corrections for previous errors
jokes.punchline: Absurd corrections treating ridiculous claims as facts they need to retract
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist take on media corrections. The specificity makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: We'd like to make the following corrections to things we said on our show.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: The following people are not, in fact, communist spies: Mayor Quimby, Chief Wiggum, and Barney Gumble.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: Apologies to the Rand Corporation, Slingerland Shoe Company, and Slom's Bake-ery. We were wrong. You are not all run by the same compassionless robots.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: And finally, the Internet is not, as we stated, 'a fun diversion.' It is, in fact, a very dangerous place where time is spent.

jokes.text: Oh, that man is sick. - Groundskeeper Willie saved you, Homer. But listen to the music. He's evil.
jokes.setup: Family watching Willie portrayed as evil on TV
jokes.punchline: Marge points out how musical scoring manipulates perception
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-commentary on how media manipulation works. Marge's media literacy moment is surprising and insightful.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: You know, it's not really fair to portray Willie as evil just because of some music. Musical scoring can really manipulate how we perceive things.

jokes.text: Hasn't this experience taught you you can't believe everything you hear? - Marge, my friend, I haven't learned a thing.
jokes.setup: Marge trying to extract a lesson from Homer's ordeal
jokes.punchline: Homer proudly admits complete inability to learn from experience
jokes.explanation: Perfect series-ending character moment. Homer's complete inability to grow is both frustrating and funny. Extremely quotable and perfectly structured.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, haven't you learned anything from this experience?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, Marge. I've learned that I never learn anything.

tmdb_overview: When Homer gropes for a gummy candy stuck to the babysitter's pants, she mistakes his intentions and turns the town against him.
insight: Sexual harassment lawsuit spawns 78 jokes at 4.15-per-minute density, fueling Season 6's sharpest episode.
